desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care.
narrative:
On October 9, 2025, from 4:00 PM until 6:00 PM, facility personnel did not contact the Attending Veterinarian with
changes in an animal's post-operative health status until the animal had died.
An adult female dog (Carmella) underwent a spay procedure in the early afternoon on October 9. According to facility
representatives, the designated veterinarian who performed surgeries on this day and other staff members monitored the
animal until 4:00 pm, during which time the animal was slow to recover but appeared stable. The surgeon left at 4:00 pm.
According to an internal investigation and conversations with facility representatives, after 4:00 PM, the dog was not
recovering as expected and close monitoring continued, but personnel did not contact a veterinarian with this update. At
5:20 pm, out of concern for the animal's condition, care staff decided to run additional diagnostics on the dog, but still did
not communicate this to the Attending Veterinarian nor to the veterinarian who had performed the surgery. Records show
that at 6:00 PM, the dog rapidly deteriorated further and care staff alerted the closest veterinarian in the building to assist
in performing CPR. The dog was not successfully resuscitated. Thereafter, care personnel contacted the Attending
Veterinarian for the first time about the animal.
Failing to contact the Attending Veterinarian in a timely manner with changes in an animal's health status can lead to
severe health outcomes.
The facility has taken corrective measures to its animal care program and SOPs after an internal investigation. From this
day forward, continue to assure that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health is conveyed to the
Attending Veterinarian so that the Attending Veterinarian can direct the facility on veterinary care.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

The gate section of the perimeter fence on the south side of the enclosure that houses the two camels and one
zebra as well as the east fence line that makes up the perimeter fence is only five feet in height. The requirement
is that the perimeter fence be six feet in height for non-dangerous type animals such as the camel and zebra. Lack
of a six foot perimeter fence does not work to restrict unwanted animals and unauthorized people from having
access to the animals housed within. It also cannot function as a secondary containment system. The areas of the
perimeter fence that are five feet in height must be corrected to six feet in height. Correct by: November 30, 2014.
Inspection and exit interview conducted with the licensee.
End of report.

desc: ACCESS AND INSPECTION OF RECORDS AND PROPERTY; SUBMISSION OF ITINERARIES.
narrative:
Required facility records (the program of veterinary care, animal inventory/acquisition records, and records of
animal dispositions) were not available for review at the time of inspection. The unavailability of the required
records does not allow APHIS Officials to determine if the records are accurate and complete, that the program of
veterinary care is being followed to ensure the health of the animals, or for the proper tracking of the animals.
Maintain all required records at the facility so they are available for review at the time of inspection. Correct from
this date forward.
Note-no regulated animals were present at the time of inspection.
This inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the licensee.
End of report.
